This binding inactivates the enzyme, and acid secretion can only resume once new proton pumps are synthesized and inserted into the cell membrane.

question:
The ridge that descends from the cuspal tip towards the central part of the occlusal surface in a maxillary molar is. Answer Choices:
A. Triangular ridge
B. Marginal ridge
C. Transverse ridge
D. Oblique ridge
label: Triangular ridge
rationale: The ridge that descends from the cuspal tip towards the central part of the occlusal surface in a maxillary molar is known as the [LABEL] of the tooth. However, it is not part of the surface of the mandible, as the mandible pertains to the lower jaw. The correct answer is that the [LABEL] is a feature of the tooth itself.

question:
Four days after being hospitalized, intubated, and mechanically ventilated, a 30-year-old man has no cough response during tracheal suctioning. He was involved in a motor vehicle collision and was obtunded on arrival in the emergency department. The ventilator is at a FiO2 of 100%, tidal volume is 920 mL, and positive end-expiratory pressure is 5 cm H2O. He is currently receiving vasopressors. His vital signs are within normal limits. The pupils are dilated and nonreactive to light. Corneal, gag, and oculovestibular reflexes are absent. There is no facial or upper extremity response to painful stimuli; the lower extremities show a triple flexion response to painful stimuli. Serum concentrations of electrolytes, urea, creatinine, and glucose are within the reference range. Arterial blood gas shows:
pH 7.45
pCO2 41 mm Hg
pO2 99 mm Hg
O2 saturation 99%
Two days ago, a CT scan of the head showed a left intracerebral hemorrhage with mass effect. The apnea test is positive. There are no known family members, advanced directives, or individuals with power of attorney. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?". Answer Choices:
A. Ethics committee consultation
B. Court order for further management
C. Remove the ventilator
D. Repeat CT scan of the head
label: Remove the ventilator
rationale: The most appropriate next step in management is to [LABEL].

question:
Indication of poor prognosis of systemic sclerosis is:. Answer Choices:
A. Calcinosis cutis
B. Renal involvement
C. Alopecia
D. Telangiectasia
label: Renal involvement
rationale: Renal insufficiency due to scleroderma renal crisis is indicative of poor prognosis in systemic sclerosis. Avoiding high doses of corticosteroids is crucial, as they are a significant risk factor for triggering renal crisis.

question:
Which is the anesthetic agent of choice in a case of status asthmaticus?. Answer Choices:
A. Ketamine
B. Thiopentone
C. Ether
D. Nitrous Oxide
label: Ketamine
rationale: [LABEL] is the anesthetic agent of choice in status asthmaticus because it causes bronchodilation through the inhibition of vagal tone, release of endogenous catecholamines, and direct relaxation of bronchial smooth muscle. Its unique properties make it highly effective in managing this life-threatening condition.

question:
Bochdalek hernia is related to -. Answer Choices:
A. Inguinal canal
B. Pleuroperitoneal canal
C. Femoral canal
D. Obturator foramen
label: Pleuroperitoneal canal
rationale: Bochdalek hernia is related to a defect in the [LABEL] during embryonic development. Its management involves addressing complications such as pulmonary hypoplasia, pulmonary arterial hypertension, and respiratory distress, followed by surgical repair after stabilization of the infant.

question:
A 70-year-old man presents to the clinic with right-sided chest pain and difficulty breathing for the past 10 days. When it began, the pain was mild, but as time went on, it increased to a level at which the man found it difficult to breathe. Two years ago, he was diagnosed with clear cell carcinoma of the kidney. Vital signs include: pulse rate is 72/min, blood pressure is 122/80 mm Hg, respiratory rate is 16/min, and temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F). On physical examination, the trachea appears to have deviated to the left, respiratory movements are diminished, there is decreased resonance on percussion, and there is an absence of breath sounds over the right hemithorax. Which of the following is the most likely clinical diagnosis in this patient?. Answer Choices:
A. Pneumonia
B. Atelectasis
C. Pneumothorax
D. Pleural effusion
label: Pleural effusion
rationale: The most likely clinical diagnosis in this patient is [LABEL], a pleural disease.

question:
Which of the following topical agent boosts cell - mediated immunity in burns. Answer Choices:
A. Mafenide acelate
B. Silver nitrate
C. Cerium nitrate
D. Povidone iodine
label: Cerium nitrate
rationale: [LABEL] is the topical agent that boosts cell-mediated immunity and forms sterile eschar in burns.

question:
A 34-year-old woman presents with acute onset loss of vision and visual disturbances. She says that, several hours ago, her vision began to get dim, and she sees halos around light sources. This was immediately followed by a severe frontal headache. Past medical history is significant for epilepsy. The patient says her anticonvulsant medication was changed recently but she doesn’t remember the name. Slit-lamp examination reveals mild chemosis, injection, and ciliary flush with diffuse stromal haze, along with very shallow peripheral anterior chambers with areas of iridocorneal touch in both eyes. Gonioscopy showed closed angles bilaterally. Which of the following antiepileptic drugs is most likely responsible for this patient’s condition?. Answer Choices:
A. Lamotrigine
B. Topiramate
C. Gabapentin
D. Tiagabine
label: Topiramate
rationale: The patient's condition is most likely angle closure glaucoma caused by the anticonvulsant medication [LABEL]. This diagnosis is supported by the clinical presentation, slit-lamp findings, gonioscopy results, and the known side effect profile of [LABEL].

question:
A 46-year-old man presents to a clinic with a complaint of intermittent flank pain bilaterally for 5 days. The pain is colicky in nature and radiates to the groin. The patient took an old prescription (hyoscyamine) and got some relief. He has nausea, but had not vomited until now. Although he has a history of renal stones, he denies any blood in the urine or stool and gives no history of fevers, changes in bowel habits, or abdominal distension. He does not have joint pain. On examination of the abdomen, the is no organomegaly and the bowel sounds are normal.
The blood test report reveals the following:
Serum calcium 8.9 mg/dL
Serum uric acid 8.9 mg/dL
Serum creatinine 1.1 mg /dL
The urinalysis shows the following:
pH 6.0
Pus cells none
RBCs 1–2/HPF
Epithelial cells 1/HPF
Protein negative
Ketones negative
Crystals oxalate (plenty)
An abdominal ultrasound shows echogenic medullary pyramids with multiple dense echogenic foci in both kidneys, that cast posterior acoustic shadows. Which of the following best describes the pathogenesis of the disease process?. Answer Choices:
A. Acquired condition secondary to dialysis
B. Developmental anomaly characterized by cystic dilatation of the collecting tubules in the renal pyramids
C. Neoplastic changes in the proximal tubular cells of the kidneys
D. Vascular anomalies and genetic mutations leading to maldevelopment of the kidneys
label: Developmental anomaly characterized by cystic dilatation of the collecting tubules in the renal pyramids
rationale: The pathogenesis of the disease process is a [LABEL], leading to renal medullary hyperechogenicity and abnormal renal echogenicity. This is consistent with medullary sponge kidney (MSK).

question:
A 23 year old male who is otherwise normal complains of mild pain in his right iliac fossa in a waveform pattern which increases during the night and he becomes exhausted and is admitted in the hospital. On examination there is mild hematuria. Urine examination reveals plenty of RBCs, 50WBCs/hpf. Urine pH is 5.5. Most likely diagnosis is:. Answer Choices:
A. Glomerulonephritis
B. Ca-Urinary bladder
C. Ureteral calculus
D. Cystitis
label: Ureteral calculus
rationale: The most likely diagnosis is [LABEL], consistent with the clinical presentation of colicky pain, hematuria, and acidic urine.

question:
A 32-year-old man with a history of alcohol binge drinking and polysubstance use is found down in his hotel room with bottles of alcohol, oxycodone, alprazolam, amphetamine-dextroamphetamine, and tadalafil. When EMS arrives, he appears comatose with pinpoint pupils and oxygen saturation of 80% on room air. He is intubated at the scene and airlifted to the nearest intensive care unit. Body temperature is 95 degrees F (35 degrees C). Creatine phosphokinase is 12,000 U/L. MRI of the brain demonstrates extensive infarcts consistent with acute hypoxic ischemic injury. Which of the following is the likely culprit for his overdose?. Answer Choices:
A. Alcohol
B. Opioids
C. Benzodiazepines
D. Amphetamines
label: Opioids
rationale: Based on the clinical presentation, the most likely cause of the overdose is [LABEL]. The combination of pinpoint pupils, hypoxemia, hypothermia, coma, and evidence of hypoxia-induced complications (elevated CK and ischemia-reperfusion injury) strongly supports this conclusion.

question:
Color of kit 3 for STD under AIDS control programme?. Answer Choices:
A. Red
B. Blue
C. White
D. Green
label: White
rationale: The correct answer is Kit 3 ([LABEL]), which is used for managing genital ulcers under the AIDS control programme.

question:
A 38-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 1, at 24 weeks' gestation comes to the physician for a routine prenatal evaluation. She has no history of major medical illness and takes no medications. Fetal ultrasonography shows a cardiac defect resulting from abnormal development of the endocardial cushions. This defect is most likely to result in which of the following?. Answer Choices:
A. Atrioventricular septal defect
B. Sinus venosus defect
C. Transposition of the great vessels
D. Dextrocardia
label: Atrioventricular septal defect
rationale: The defect is most likely an [LABEL] (AVSD), which is a type of heart septal defect caused by abnormal development of the endocardial cushions.

question:
A 66-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department 4 hours after falling and hitting her head while skiing. Initially, she refused treatment, but an hour ago she began to develop a severe headache, nausea, and right leg weakness. She has osteopenia. Her only medication is a daily multivitamin. She has no visual changes and is oriented to person, time, and place. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 72/min, respirations are 18/min and regular, and blood pressure is 128/75 mm Hg. Examination shows a 5-cm bruise on the left side of her skull. The pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light and accommodation. Muscle strength is 0/5 in her right knee and foot. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?. Answer Choices:
A. Tonsillar herniation
B. Uncal herniation
C. Subfalcine herniation
D. Extracranial herniation
label: Subfalcine herniation
rationale: The most likely cause of this patient's symptoms is [LABEL], which can result in contralateral limb muscle weakness due to compression of motor pathways.

question:
A 26-year-old woman comes to the physician because of recurrent thoughts that cause her severe distress. She describes these thoughts as gory images of violent people entering her flat with criminal intent. She has had tremors and palpitations while experiencing these thoughts and must get up twenty to thirty times at night to check that the door and windows have been locked. She says that neither the thoughts nor her actions are consistent with her “normal self”. She has a history of general anxiety disorder and major depressive disorder. She drinks 1–2 alcoholic beverages weekly and does not smoke or use illicit drugs. She takes no medications. She appears healthy and well nourished. Her vital signs are within normal limits. On mental status examination, she is calm, alert and oriented to person, place, and time. She describes her mood as ""good.""; her speech is organized, logical, and coherent. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?". Answer Choices:
A. Olanzapine
B. Venlafaxine
C. Risperidone
D. Sertraline
label: Sertraline
rationale: The most appropriate next step in management is initiating treatment with [LABEL], as it is a first-line treatment for PTSD, anxiety disorders, and depression, all of which are relevant to the patient's presentation.

question:
A 25-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 30 minutes after he was involved in a motorcycle collision. He was not wearing a helmet. Physical examination shows left periorbital ecchymosis. A CT scan of the head shows a fracture of the greater wing of the left sphenoid bone with compression of the left superior orbital fissure. Physical examination of this patient is most likely to show which of the following findings?. Answer Choices:
A. Decreased sense of smell
B. Numbness of the left cheek
C. Preserved left lateral gaze
D. Absent left corneal reflex
label: Absent left corneal reflex
rationale: Based on the reasoning process, the physical examination of this patient is most likely to show a decreased corneal reflex on the left side due to the involvement of the ophthalmic branch of the trigeminal nerve (CN V1) as it passes through the compressed superior orbital fissure.

question:
A 59-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of abdominal pain and bloody diarrhea that began 12 hours ago. Three days ago, she ate undercooked chicken at a local restaurant. Blood cultures grow spiral and comma-shaped, oxidase-positive organisms at 42°C. This patient is at greatest risk for which of the following complications?. Answer Choices:
A. Segmental myelin degeneration
B. Seizures
C. Erythema nodosum
D. Peyer patch necrosis
label: Segmental myelin degeneration
rationale: This patient is at greatest risk for Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS), which involves segmental peripheral demyelination and remyelination. The infection with *Campylobacter jejuni* is the likely trigger for this immune-mediated condition.

question:
A 42-year-old man presents to his family physician for evaluation of oral pain. He states that he has increasing pain in a molar on the top left of his mouth. The pain started 1 week ago and has been progressively worsening since then. His medical history is significant for hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus, both of which are currently controlled with lifestyle modifications. His blood pressure is 124/86 mm Hg, heart rate is 86/min, and respiratory rate is 14/min. Physical examination is notable for a yellow-black discoloration of the second molar on his left upper mouth. The decision is made to refer him to a dentist for further management of this cavity. The patient has never had any dental procedures and is nervous about what type of sedation will be used. Which of the following forms of anesthesia utilizes solely an oral or intravenous anti-anxiety medication?. Answer Choices:
A. Minimal Sedation
B. Epidural anesthesia
C. Deep sedation
D. Regional anesthesia
label: Minimal Sedation
rationale: [LABEL], often achieved using benzodiazepines, is a form of anesthesia that utilizes oral or intravenous anti-anxiety medication. This is appropriate for managing anxiety and ensuring comfort during dental procedures.

question:
Commonest presentation of neurocysticercosis is:. Answer Choices:
A. Seizures
B. Focal neurological deficits
C. Dementia
D. Radiculopathy
label: Seizures
rationale: [LABEL] are a common manifestation of neurocysticercosis due to the localization of cysts in the central nervous system and the resulting inflammatory response, which can disrupt normal brain function. This aligns with the understanding that [LABEL] occur in a significant proportion of cases.

question:
A 27-year-old woman presents with a history of repeated episodes of discoloration of the fingers over the last 3 years. She mentions that the episodes are usually triggered by exposure to cold, which leads to a sequential white, blue, and red discoloration of her fingers, followed by resolution of the symptoms. During an episode, she experiences pain and numbness in the affected fingers. The episodes are usually of short duration and do not interfere with her life, so she did not seek medical advice till now. Which of the following additional clinical features in this patient would most likely support the most likely diagnosis?. Answer Choices:
A. Calcinosis on the dorsal surface of the forearm
B. Generalized pruritus
C. Telangiectasia over face
D. Bilateral symmetrical involvement of the extremities
label: Bilateral symmetrical involvement of the extremities
rationale: [LABEL] strongly supports the diagnosis of Raynaud's phenomenon, likely primary Raynaud's in this case, given the absence of systemic symptoms or complications.

question:
Triamterene causes. Answer Choices:
A. Better glucose tolerance
B. Muscle cramps
C. Decrease in urea level
D. Hypokalemia
label: Muscle cramps
rationale: Triamterene can cause [LABEL] as a side effect. This is supported by its mechanism of action (inhibition of renal sodium ion transport leading to electrolyte imbalances) and the explicit listing of [LABEL] as a known side effect.

question:
A four-year-old boy is brought to his pediatrician by his mother for recurrent nosebleeds. The mother reports that the boy has had five nosebleeds within the past 2 weeks, each lasting between 15 and 20 minutes. The patient was born at term and has been hospitalized twice for pneumonia treatment. There is no family history of serious illness. The patient is at the 8th percentile for height and the 30th percentile for weight. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows a small, thin child with two flat, dark brown areas of hyperpigmentation across the upper back and a similar discoloration on the left buttock. There is bilateral esotropia. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin concentration of 9.3 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 107 μm3, leukocyte count of 3,800/mm3, and platelet count of 46,000/mm3. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's condition?. Answer Choices:
A. Defect in DNA crosslink repair
B. Mutation in WAS protein
C. Recent history of NSAID use
D. Postviral autoimmune reaction
label: Defect in DNA crosslink repair
rationale: The most likely underlying cause of this patient's condition is a [LABEL], consistent with Fanconi anemia.

question:
A 30 year old male was brought for evaluation. The history revealed that the patients 3 year old son died, 5 months back after being hit by a car. At the time of accident, patient was standing nearby and witnessed the accident. For last 5 months, he has been having symptoms of sadness of mood, crying spells, feelings of wohlessness, poor sleep and poor appetite. He has twice thought of killing himself but stopped at the end moment. He has not been attending the office of last 5 months. What is the likely diagnosis:. Answer Choices:
A. Post traumatic stress disorder
B. Normal grief
C. Major depression
D. Adjustment disorder
label: Major depression
rationale: The most likely diagnosis is major depressive disorder (MDD). The patient’s symptoms meet the diagnostic criteria for MDD, including depressed mood, poor appetite, poor sleep, feelings of worthlessness, and suicidal ideation, persisting for more than 2 weeks and causing significant functional impairment.

question:
Which of the following is a structural, fibrous protein found in the dermis?. Answer Choices:
A. Collagen
B. Heparin
C. Lipocyte
D. Melanin
label: Collagen
rationale: [LABEL] is a structural, fibrous protein found in the dermis. It is the most abundant and critical protein for providing tensile strength and structural support to the skin.

question:
A patient presents with Hb of 8 gm% , WBC count of 2000/mm3 and platlet count of 60000/mm3. What is your likely diagnosis?. Answer Choices:
A. Thalassemia
B. Sickle cell anemia
C. Aplastic anemia
D. Anemia of chronic disease
label: Aplastic anemia
rationale: The most likely diagnosis is [LABEL], characterized by pancytopenia (anemia, leukopenia, and thrombocytopenia) due to bone marrow failure.

question:
A 45-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of hematuria and bilateral flank pain. He has passed urinary stones twice before and has a history of recurrent urinary tract infections. He reports no recent trauma. His father had a history of kidney failure and underwent a kidney transplant. His temperature is 38.0°C (100.4°F), pulse is 110/min, and blood pressure is 155/98 mm Hg. Abdominal examination shows palpable, bilateral flank masses. Results of a complete blood count are within the reference range. His serum creatinine concentration is 2.9 mg/dL. Which of the following findings is most likely to be associated with this patient's condition?. Answer Choices:
A. Vesicoureteral reflux
B. Portal hypertension
C. Osteolytic bone lesions
D. Cerebral saccular aneurysm
label: Cerebral saccular aneurysm
rationale: The patient's condition is most likely polycystic kidney disease, which is associated with an increased risk of [LABEL]s. This explains the symptoms, family history, and laboratory findings.

question:
All are true regarding paroxysmal nocturnal haemoglobinuria, except-. Answer Choices:
A. Haemosiderinuria
B. Pancytopenia
C. Increased alkaline phosphatase
D. Cellular marrow
label: Increased alkaline phosphatase
rationale: The statement "[LABEL] activity is not true regarding paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria" is correct. Elevated ALP activity is not a characteristic of PNH, as the condition primarily affects the blood and complement system, not the liver, bones, or biliary system.

question:
A new mother brings in her 2-week-old son because of a painful itchy rash on his trunk. Vital signs are within normal limits. A basic chemistry panel reveal sodium 135 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, chloride 107 mmol/L, carbon dioxide 22, blood urea nitrogen 30 mg/dL, creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, and glucose 108 mg/dL. On physical examination of the newborn, there are confluent erythematous patches with tiny vesicles and scaling. His mother notes that she has been bathing the patient twice a day. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?. Answer Choices:
A. Impetigo
B. Atopic dermatitis
C. Eczema herpeticum
D. Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome
label: Atopic dermatitis
rationale: The most likely diagnosis for the newborn is [LABEL].

question:
A 44-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with complaints of fatigue, muscle weakness, cramps, and increased urination over the past several weeks. His past medical history is significant only for hypertension, for which he was started on hydrochlorothiazide (HCTZ) 4 weeks ago. Vital signs at today's visit are as follows: T 37.2, HR 88, BP 129/80, RR 14, and SpO2 99%. Physical examination does not reveal any abnormal findings. Serologic studies are significant for a serum potassium level of 2.1 mEq/L (normal range 3.5-5.0 mEq/L). Lab-work from his last visit showed a basic metabolic panel and complete blood count results to all be within normal limits. Which of the following underlying diseases most likely contributed to the development of this patient's presenting condition?. Answer Choices:
A. Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone Secretion (SIADH)
B. Pituitary adenoma
C. Hyperaldosteronism
D. Cushing's disease
label: Hyperaldosteronism
rationale: The patient's condition is most likely due to primary [LABEL], which has been exacerbated by the use of hydrochlorothiazide. The [LABEL] explains the hypertension, severe hypokalemia, and associated symptoms such as muscle weakness, cramps, and increased urination.

question:
Assessment of obesity by following measurement except. Answer Choices:
A. Quetelet’s index
B. Broca index
C. Sullivan’s index
D. Corpulence index
label: Sullivan’s index
rationale: [LABEL] is not used for the assessment of obesity. It is a metric related to life expectancy and health status, not body fat or obesity evaluation. Therefore, the statement in the question is correct.

question:
A 50 years old man was recently diagnosed to be having coronary artery disease. There was no added risk factors except for a LDL value of 150-165mgs/dl. The single drug most appropriate for initial therapy is. Answer Choices:
A. Gemfibrozil
B. Nicotinic acid
C. Bile acid binding resins
D. Statins (Any)
label: Statins (Any)
rationale: The most appropriate single drug for initial therapy in this case is a high-intensity statin, such as Atorvastatin or Rosuvastatin. These drugs are effective in lowering LDL cholesterol and reducing cardiovascular risk in patients with coronary artery disease.

question:
All of the following are features if CD4 count drop below 50cells/ mmcubic, except. Answer Choices:
A. HIV associated dementia.
B. CMV retinitis.
C. Disseminated mycobacterium avium intracellulare.
D. None.
label: HIV associated dementia
rationale: HIV-associated dementia (AIDS dementia complex) is a feature that can occur in patients with CD4 counts below 50 cells/mm³, as this level of immunosuppression increases the risk of CNS involvement by HIV. The given answer stating that "HIV-associated dementia is not a feature when CD4 count drops below 50 cells/mm³" appears to be incorrect.

question:
Defect in Snurps causes-. Answer Choices:
A. Sickle cell anemia
B. Thalassemia
C. Marfan syndrome
D. EDS
label: Thalassemia
rationale: Defective splicing due to a defect in snurps is not the most common mutation leading to [LABEL]. The most common causes are point mutations (e.g., nonsense or frameshift mutations) for beta-[LABEL] and gene deletions for alpha-[LABEL]. While snurp defects could theoretically contribute, they are not the primary mechanism.

question:
A 16-month-old girl is brought to the emergency department by emergency medical technicians because of a generalized tonic-clonic seizure that began 25 minutes ago and has continued despite administration of 0.5 mg/kg rectal diazepam 10 minutes ago. The patient was at home at the time of the seizure. In the emergency department, she is given an additional 0.5 mg/kg dose of diazepam intravenously and the convulsion terminates. Within 2 minutes, her oxygen saturation drops to 75% and she appears cyanotic. Respirations are 10/min and shallow. She receives bag-valve-mask ventilation, followed by intubation and mechanical ventilation. Medical history is unremarkable. She takes no medications. Temperature is 39.1°C (102.5°F). Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of the patient's respiratory insufficiency?. Answer Choices:
A. Airway occlusion
B. Encephalitis
C. Medication-induced suppression of central respiratory drive
D. Meningitis
label: Medication-induced suppression of central respiratory drive
rationale: The most likely underlying cause of the patient's respiratory insufficiency is [LABEL] due to diazepam, leading to hypoventilation and decreased sensitivity to hypoxemia.

question:
A 45-year-old woman presents with heavy menstrual bleeding between her periods. The patient also complains of experiencing an irregular menstrual cycle, weight loss, bloating, and constipation. She has had 3 uncomplicated pregnancies, all of which ended with normal vaginal deliveries at term. She has never taken oral contraception, and she does not take any medication at the time of presentation. She has no family history of any gynecological malignancy; however, her grandfather and mother had colon cancer that was diagnosed before they turned 50. On physical examination, the patient appears pale. Gynecological examination reveals a bloody cervical discharge and slight uterine enlargement. Endometrial biopsy reveals endometrial adenocarcinoma. Colonoscopy reveals several polyps located in the ascending colon, which are shown to be adenocarcinomas on histological evaluation. Which of the following mechanisms of DNA repair is likely to be disrupted in this patient?. Answer Choices:
A. Nucleotide-excision repair
B. Base-excision repair
C. Mismatch repair
D. Non-homologous end joining
label: Mismatch repair
rationale: The likely disrupted mechanism of DNA repair in this patient is [LABEL].

question:
The chyle from intestine is rich with chylomicrons. Which of the following form the protein core of chylomicrons?. Answer Choices:
A. Triglyceride only
B. Triglyceride + cholesterol
C. Triglyceride + cholesterol + phospholipid
D. Only cholesterol
label: Triglyceride + cholesterol + phospholipid
rationale: The protein core of chylomicrons is not formed by triglyceride, cholesterol, and phospholipid. Instead, the core is primarily lipid-based (triacylglycerols and cholesterol esters), while apoproteins like APOA1 are located on the surface. The given answer is incorrect.

question:
Risk of mother to child HIV transmission in pregnant woman at the time of delivery, and after delivery in non breast feeding woman is. Answer Choices:
A. 5-10%
B. 15-30%
C. 10-15%
D. More than 50%
rationale: The risk of mother-to-child HIV transmission in a pregnant woman at the time of delivery and after delivery (in a non-breastfeeding context) is approximately [LABEL], with variations depending on the healthcare setting and geographic region. This aligns with the understanding that the absence of intervention leads to significant transmission risk, particularly during delivery.

question:
A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic complaining of fatigue and nausea. She was recently diagnosed with metastatic ovarian cancer and subsequently started treatment with an alkylating agent. The patient reports recent weight loss, malaise, and drowsiness, but denies fever, headaches, palpitations, shortness of breath, or genitourinary symptoms. Physical exam was unremarkable. Laboratory tests are shown below.

Serum:
Na+: 137 mEq/L

K+: 4.8 mEq/L
HCO3-: 25 mEq/L
BUN: 8.5 mg/dL
Glucose: 117 mg/dL
Creatinine: 2.1 mg/dL
Thyroid-stimulating hormone: 1.8 µU/mL
Ca2+: 9.6 mg/dL
AST: 8 U/L
ALT: 11 U/L

What findings on urinalysis are most specific to this patient’s diagnosis?. Answer Choices:
A. Elevated protein
B. Granular casts
C. Normal findings
D. WBC casts
label: Granular casts
rationale: Based on the reasoning process, [LABEL] on urinalysis are indeed a plausible finding in this patient due to the potential nephrotoxic effects of the alkylating agent used in her cancer treatment. This aligns with the symptoms and the clinical context of renal involvement.

question:
An 11-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with sudden and severe pain in the left scrotum that started 2 hours ago. He has vomited twice. He has no dysuria or frequency. There is no history of trauma to the testicles. The temperature is 37.7°C (99.9°F). The left scrotum is swollen, erythematous, and tender. The left testis is elevated and swollen with a transverse lie. The cremasteric reflex is absent. Ultrasonographic examination is currently pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?. Answer Choices:
A. Epididymitis
B. Germ cell tumor
C. Mumps orchitis
D. Testicular torsion
label: Testicular torsion
rationale: The most likely diagnosis is [LABEL], characterized by spermatic cord torsion, acute scrotal pain, an absent cremasteric reflex, and the described physical findings. This is a urological emergency requiring immediate surgical intervention to restore blood flow and prevent testicular loss.

question:
A 23-year-old woman presents with ongoing diplopia for 1 week. She has noticed that her diplopia is more prominent when she looks at objects in her periphery. It does not present when looking straight ahead. She does not have a fever, headache, ocular pain, lacrimation, blurring of vision, or changes in her color vision. She is a college student and is otherwise healthy. The neurological examination reveals that when she looks to the left, her right eye does not adduct while her left eye abducts with nystagmus. Furthermore, when she looks to the right, her left eye does not adduct while her right eye abducts with prominent nystagmus. Her pupils are bilateral, equal and reactive to light and accommodation. The convergence is normal. The rest of the cranial nerve examination is unremarkable. What is the next best step in the management of this patient?. Answer Choices:
A. Computed tomography (CT) scan of the head
B. Lumbar puncture
C. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain
D. Ophthalmology referral
label: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain
rationale: The next best step in the management of this patient with suspected internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO) is to perform [LABEL].

question:
2 hours after being admitted to the hospital because of a fracture of the right ankle, a 75-year-old man continues to complain of pain despite treatment with acetaminophen and ibuprofen. He has a history of dementia and cannot recall his medical history. The presence of which of the following features would most likely be a reason to avoid treatment with morphine in this patient?. Answer Choices:
A. Watery diarrhea
B. Biliary tract dysfunction
C. Persistent cough
D. Tachypnea
label: Biliary tract dysfunction
rationale: Based on the reasoning process, [LABEL] would indeed be a reason to avoid treatment with morphine in this patient. This conclusion aligns with the understanding that morphine can cause biliary tract spasm, which could be problematic if the patient has underlying biliary tract issues.

question:
Narcotic overdose can be antagonized by. Answer Choices:
A. Diphenhydramine
B. Atropine
C. Naloxane
D. Nalorphine
label: Naloxane
rationale: Naloxone is used to antagonize narcotic overdose by reversing the effects of opioids on the central nervous system and respiratory system, making it an effective treatment for acute opioid poisoning.

question:
A 49-year-old man presents to a new primary care provider complaining of fatigue and occasional fever over the last month. These symptoms are starting to affect his job and he would like treatment. The physician runs a standard metabolic panel that shows elevated AST and ALT. The patient is then tested for hepatitis viruses. He is hepatitis C positive. The patient and his doctor discuss treatment options and agree upon pegylated interferon and oral ribavirin. Which side-effect is most likely while taking the ribavirin?. Answer Choices:
A. Hemolytic anemia
B. Drug-associated lupus
C. Hyperthyroidism
D. Rash
label: Hemolytic anemia
rationale: The most likely side effect while taking ribavirin is [LABEL]. This conclusion is supported by the known pharmacological effects of ribavirin and its well-documented association with [LABEL].

question:
A 62-year-old man comes to the physician for a follow-up examination. One month ago, therapy with lisinopril was initiated for treatment of hypertension. His blood pressure is 136/86 mm Hg. Urinalysis shows a creatinine clearance of 92 mL/min. The patient's serum creatinine concentration is most likely closest to which of the following values?. Answer Choices:
A. 2.3 mg/dL
B. 2.0 mg/dL
C. 1.1 mg/dL
D. 1.7 mg/dL
label: 1.1 mg/dL
rationale: The patient’s serum creatinine concentration is elevated and most likely closest to [LABEL], based on the Cockcroft-Gault equation and the reported creatinine clearance of 92 mL/min. This mild elevation is likely due to the initiation of lisinopril therapy, which can cause a small, expected increase in serum creatinine without indicating significant renal dysfunction.

question:
After an initial assessment in the emergency department, the patient is sent for an urgent CT scan of the head. CT scan reveals a mild hypodensity in the left cerebellum. What is the most likely etiology/cause?. Answer Choices:
A. Arterial dissection
B. Cardiac emboli
C. Carotid stenosis
D. Lacunar infarction
label: Arterial dissection
rationale: The most likely etiology for the mild hypodensity in the left hemisphere of the cerebellum is cervical artery dissection, as it can lead to ischemia in the posterior circulation, which supplies the cerebellum.

question:
A 24-year-old Turkish female presents to your office for a routine examination. She recently started a new job and has been tired most of the time. She does not have any dizziness and has not lost consciousness. She follows a well-balanced diet and is not vegetarian. She recalls that other family members have had similar symptoms in the past. On physical exam her temperature is 99°F (37.2°C), blood pressure is 115/78 mmHg, pulse is 100/min, respirations are 22/min, and pulse oximetry is 99% on room air. On physical exam, you notice conjunctival pallor. Labs are obtained and the results are shown below:

Hemoglobin: 10.2 g/dL
Hematocrit: 34%
Leukocyte count: 5,000 cells/mm^3 with normal differential
Platelet count: 252,000/mm^3
Mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration: 20.4%
Mean corpuscular volume: 65 µm^3

Peripheral blood smear is shown in the image provided. The cause of her anemia is most likely associated with which of the following?. Answer Choices:
A. Point mutation on chromosome 11
B. X-linked defect in ALA synthase
C. Inhibition of ALA dehydratase
D. Blood loss
label: Point mutation on chromosome 11
rationale: The most likely cause of this patient’s anemia is beta-thalassemia minor, which is associated with a [LABEL]. This aligns with her family history, microcytic anemia, and ethnic background.

question:
A 45-year-old homeless man comes to the emergency department because of a 1-week history of a red, itchy rash on his hands. He says the itching is worse at night and often wakes him from sleep. Physical examination shows the findings in the photograph. A topical drug with which of the following mechanisms of action is most likely to be effective?. Answer Choices:
A. Binding to sodium channels
B. Inhibition of nuclear factor-κB
C. Inhibition of histamine-1 receptors
D. Increase in keratinocyte turnover
label: Binding to sodium channels
rationale: A topical drug that works by [LABEL], such as permethrin, is most likely to be effective in treating the patient's condition, which is suggestive of scabies.

question:
Secondary hemorrhage is after how many days of tonsillectomy. Answer Choices:
A. Within 6 hrs of operation
B. Within 24 hrs of operation
C. Within 3 weeks of operation
D. Within 3 months of operation
label: Within 3 weeks of operation
rationale: Secondary hemorrhage after tonsillectomy typically occurs within 3 weeks of the operation, with the highest risk between the 5th and 10th days. This is due to infection-induced inflammation, tissue sloughing, and premature membrane disassembly, which expose fragile blood vessels and disrupt the healing process.

question:
A 23-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 1-year history of episodic shortness of breath. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show elevated serum IgE levels. Microscopic examination of the sputum shows eosinophilic, hexagonal, double-pointed crystals. A methacholine challenge test is positive. Exposure to which of the following is most likely responsible for this patient's condition?. Answer Choices:
A. Aspirin
B. Cold air
C. Dust mites
D. Tobacco smoke
label: Dust mites
rationale: Exposure to [LABEL] is most likely responsible for this patient's condition, as the symptoms and test results are consistent with allergic asthma triggered by common environmental allergens such as [LABEL].

question:
A healthy 28-year-old woman at 30-weeks gestational age, has gained 35lbs since becoming pregnant. She complains of several weeks of bilateral numbness and tingling of her palms, thumbs, index and middle fingers that is worse at night. She also notes weakness gripping objects at the office. Which nerve is most likely affected?. Answer Choices:
A. Median nerve
B. Ulnar nerve
C. Radial nerve
D. Anterior interosseous nerve
label: Median nerve
rationale: The [LABEL] is most likely affected, leading to the symptoms of numbness, tingling, and weakness in the hands, consistent with carpal tunnel syndrome.

question:
The rate-limiting step in the synthesis of coisol is catalyzed by. Answer Choices:
A. 21-Hydroxylase
B. 3b-Hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase
C. Cholesterol side-chain cleavage enzyme
D. 11b-Hydroxylase
label: Cholesterol side-chain cleavage enzyme
rationale: The enzyme responsible for the rate-limiting step in the steroid biosynthetic process, specifically with cholesterol monooxygenase (side-chain-cleaving) activity, is CYP11A1 ([LABEL]). This enzyme is critical for converting cholesterol into pregnenolone, the precursor for all steroid hormones.

question:
Delta bilirubin is ______ bilirubin _____ bound to albumin.. Answer Choices:
A. Conjugated, covalently
B. Unconjugated, non-covalently
C. Conjugated, noncovalently
D. Unconjugated, covalently
label: Conjugated, covalently
rationale: Delta bilirubin is conjugated bilirubin covalently bound to albumin. This covalent binding explains its role in persistent hyperbilirubinemia in cases of obstructive jaundice, as delta bilirubin is not readily excreted and remains in the bloodstream even after jaundice resolves.

question:
At a counseling session, a 15-year-old boy recounts his childhood. He explained that his father was an angry, violent man who physically abused him and his younger brother every time he was drunk - which was almost every night. The boy said that the only way he could escape the situation was to believe that he himself was a superhero that would fight crime. Which of the following best describes the ego defense of this male?. Answer Choices:
A. Dissociation
B. Isolation of affect
C. Fantasy
D. Splitting
label: Fantasy
rationale: The ego defense mechanism used by the boy is best described as [LABEL], as he coped with the trauma of abuse by imagining himself as a superhero fighting crime.

question:
A 6-year-old boy presents to his primary care physician with hip pain that started this morning. The patient claims the pain is severe and is stopping him from skateboarding. The patient recently recovered from a upper respiratory infection that he caught from his siblings but has otherwise been healthy. The patient has a past medical history of obesity. His temperature is 98.1°F (36.7°C), blood pressure is 100/55 mmHg, pulse is 90/min, respirations are 22/min, and oxygen saturation is 98% on room air. On physical exam, you note an obese boy in no acute distress. Cardiopulmonary exam is within normal limits. Inspection of the hip reveals no abnormalities or swelling. The hip exhibits a normal range of motion and physical exam only elicits minor pain. The patient's gait appears normal and pain is elicited when the patient jumps or runs. Which of the following is the best next step in management for this patient's most likely diagnosis?. Answer Choices:
A. Radiography
B. MRI
C. Aspiration and broad spectrum antibiotics
D. Ibuprofen and rest
label: Ibuprofen and rest
rationale: The best next step in management for this patient’s most likely diagnosis (transient synovitis) is [LABEL]. This approach is consistent with the clinical presentation and the self-limiting nature of the condition.

question:
A 9-month-old boy is brought to the pediatrician because he can not sit on his own without support and has involuntary movements. He was born vaginally with no complications at full term. There is no history of consanguinity among parents. On physical examination, it was noticed that he is a stunted infant with generalized hypotonia and severe generalized dystonic movements. The mother says that she has noticed the presence of orange sand in his diapers many times. Laboratory evaluation revealed elevated uric acid levels in both blood and urine. Hypoxanthine-guanine phosphoribosyltransferase is found to be deficient in his blood samples. He was prescribed an appropriate medication and sent home. The most likely mechanism of this drug is the inhibition of which of the following enzymes in addition to xanthine oxidase?. Answer Choices:
A. Purine nucleoside phosphorylase
B. Orotate phosphoribosyltransferase
C. Ribonucleotide reductase
D. Dihydrofolate reductase
label: Purine nucleoside phosphorylase
rationale: The prescribed medication likely inhibits purine-nucleoside phosphorylase (PNP) activity in addition to xanthine oxidase activity. This dual inhibition would address both hyperuricemia and the underlying metabolic dysfunction in Lesch-Nyhan syndrome.

question:
Drugs used for anaesthesia in hypotensive is. Answer Choices:
A. Propofol
B. Ketamine
C. Thiopentone
D. None
label: Ketamine
rationale: [LABEL] is the induction agent of choice in acute hypotension and shock due to its ability to increase blood pressure and maintain hemodynamic stability.

question:
N. gonorrhea causes -. Answer Choices:
A. Urethritis
B. Salpingitis
C. Conjunctivitis
D. All
label: All
rationale: N. gonorrhea can cause urethritis, cervicitis, and salpingitis. These conditions are consistent with the bacterium's ability to infect and inflame various parts of the reproductive tract.

question:
Scientists are developing a new non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug for osteoarthritis. Their hope is that the new drug will have a higher potency but the same efficacy as ibuprofen in the hope of minimizing gastrointestinal side effects. If ibuprofen is curve C in the figure provided, which of the following would be the curve for the new drug based on the scientists’ specifications? The desired therapeutic effect in patients is represented by the dashed line Y.. Answer Choices:
A. Curve A
B. Curve B
C. Curve C
D. Curve D
label: Curve A
rationale: The new drug, designed to have higher potency and the same efficacy as ibuprofen, would correspond to a curve that is shifted to the left of ibuprofen's curve (indicating higher potency) but reaches the same maximum height (indicating the same efficacy). Based on this reasoning, [LABEL] is the correct representation.

question:
A 19-year-old college student is brought to the emergency department with persistent vomiting overnight. He spent all day drinking beer yesterday at a college party according to his friends. He appears to be in shock and when asked about vomiting, he says that he vomited up blood about an hour ago. At the hospital, his vomit contains streaks of blood. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), respirations are 15/min, pulse is 107/min, and blood pressure is 90/68 mm Hg. A physical examination is performed and is within normal limits. Intravenous fluids are started and a blood sample is drawn for typing and cross-matching. An immediate upper gastrointestinal endoscopy reveals a longitudinal mucosal tear in the distal esophagus. What is the most likely diagnosis?. Answer Choices:
A. Boerhaave syndrome
B. Mallory-Weiss tear
C. Pill esophagitis
D. Dieulafoy's lesion
label: Mallory-Weiss tear
rationale: The most likely diagnosis is Mallory-Weiss syndrome, which is associated with a longitudinal mucosal tear in the lower esophagus caused by persistent vomiting.

question:
A 26-year-old man is brought to the hospital by his wife who complains that her husband has been behaving oddly for the past few hours. The patient’s wife says that she has known him for only 4 months. The wife is unable to give any past medical history. The patient’s speech is difficult to follow, and he seems very distracted. After 15 minutes, he becomes agitated and starts to bang his head on a nearby pillar. He is admitted to the psychiatric ward and is given an emergency medication, after which he calms down. In the next 2 days, he continues to become agitated at times and required 2 more doses of the same drug. On the 4th day of admission, he appears very weak, confused, and does not respond to questions appropriately. His vital signs include: temperature 40.0°C (104.0°F), blood pressure 160/95 mm Hg, and pulse 114/min. On physical examination, he is profusely diaphoretic. He is unable to stand upright or even get up from his bed. Which of the following is the mechanism of action of the drug which most likely caused this patient’s current condition?. Answer Choices:
A. Dopamine receptor blocking
B. Serotonin reuptake inhibition
C. Agonistic effect on dopamine receptors
D. Skeletal muscle relaxation
label: Dopamine receptor blocking
rationale: The mechanism of action of the drug that most likely caused this patient’s current condition is D2 dopamine receptor binding. This aligns with the use of antipsychotic medications, which are known to block D2 receptors and can lead to neuroleptic malignant syndrome in rare cases.

question:
Commonest tumor of ovary occurring in a young woman is :. Answer Choices:
A. Immature teratoma
B. Yolk sac tumor
C. Endometroid tumor
D. Dysgerminoma
label: Dysgerminoma
rationale: Based on the reasoning process, [LABEL] is indeed the most common malignant ovarian tumor in young women. Therefore, the conclusion aligns with the initial answer provided.

question:
Testicular tumour most sensitive to radiation is-. Answer Choices:
A. Teratoma
B. Lymphoma
C. Mixed germ cell tumor
D. Seminoma
label: Seminoma
rationale: [LABEL] is the testicular germ cell tumor most sensitive to radiation. This conclusion is supported by its biological behavior, clinical response to radiation therapy, and distinct histological characteristics.

question:
A 47-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 3-week history of a dry cough. She does not smoke or use illicit drugs. Physical examination shows mild conjunctival hyperemia. Chest auscultation shows fine crackles in both lung fields. Laboratory studies show a total calcium concentration of 10.8 mg/dL. The results of spirometry are shown (dashed loop shows normal for comparison). Further evaluation of this patient is most likely to show an increase in which of the following?. Answer Choices:
A. Monoclonal IgG titers
B. Neutrophil elastase activity
C. Angiotensin-converting enzyme activity
D. Cold agglutinin titers
"
label: Angiotensin-converting enzyme activity
rationale: Based on the patient's symptoms (dry cough, crackles, conjunctival hyperemia), laboratory findings (hypercalcemia), and abnormal spirometry, the most likely diagnosis is sarcoidosis. Further evaluation is most likely to show an increase in angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) activity, which is a hallmark of sarcoidosis.

question:
An investigator is studying the growth of an organism in different media. The organism is inoculated on a petri dish that contains heated sheep blood, vancomycin, nystatin, trimethoprim, and colistin. The resulting growth medium is incubated at 37°C. Numerous small, white colonies are seen after incubation for 48 hours. This organism is most likely to cause which of the following conditions?. Answer Choices:
A. Pontiac fever
B. Hemolytic uremic syndrome
C. Oral thrush
D. Gonorrhea
label: Gonorrhea
rationale: The organism is most likely Neisseria gonorrhoeae, the causative agent of [LABEL].

question:
Stomach is derived from ?. Answer Choices:
A. Foregut
B. Midgut
C. Hindgut
D. Allantois
label: Foregut
rationale: The stomach is derived from the [LABEL].

question:
A 54-year-old male presents to the emergency department after an episode of bloody vomiting. He is a chronic alcoholic with a history of cirrhosis, and this is the third time he is presenting with this complaint. His first two episodes of hematemesis required endoscopic management of bleeding esophageal varices. His hemoglobin on admission laboratory evaluation was 11.2 g/dL. The patient is stabilized, and upper endoscopy is performed with successful banding of bleeding varices. Follow-up lab-work shows hemoglobin levels of 10.9 g/dL and 11.1 g/dL on days 1 and 2 after admission. Which of the following is the best next step in the management of this patient?. Answer Choices:
A. Monitor stability and discharge with continuation of endoscopic surveillance at regular 3 month intervals
B. Discuss with the patient the option of a transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic stent (TIPS)
C. Begin long-term octreotide and a 4-week course of prophylactic antibiotics
D. Give 2 units packed RBCs
label: Discuss with the patient the option of a transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic stent (TIPS)
rationale: The best next step in management is to discuss with the patient the option of a transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS) to address complications related to portal hypertension and prevent further episodes of variceal bleeding.

question:
True about Wilsons disease is?. Answer Choices:
A. Increased serum ceruloplasmin
B. Decreased liver copper
C. Increased urinary copper excretion
D. Decreased urine copper excretion
label: Increased urinary copper excretion
rationale: Increased urinary copper concentration is indeed a characteristic feature of Wilson disease. This is due to the impaired copper metabolism caused by ATP7B mutations, leading to copper accumulation in the liver and subsequent excretion through the kidneys.

question:
A 10-year-old boy developed hoarseness of voice following an attack of diphtheria. On examination his right vocal was paralysed. The treatment of choice for paralysed vocal cord will be:. Answer Choices:
A. Gel foam injection of right vocal cord
B. Fat injection of right vocal cord
C. Thyroplasty type-I
D. Wait for spontaneous recovery of vocal cord
label: Wait for spontaneous recovery of vocal cord
rationale: The treatment of choice for vocal cord paralysis following diphtheria is to wait for spontaneous recovery, as nerve regeneration may occur in a few weeks or months. If recovery does not take place, compensation by the left vocal cord is possible.

question:
Which of the following beta adrenergic agonist is used as an aerosol for treatment of bronchial asthma?. Answer Choices:
A. Salbutamol
B. Ketotifen
C. Fluticasone
D. Monteleukast
label: Salbutamol
rationale: [LABEL] is the beta-2 adrenergic agonist used as an aerosol for the treatment of bronchial asthma.

question:
Once the enamel formation is complete, the tooth will erupt after approximately:. Answer Choices:
A. One year
B. Two years
C. Three years
D. Four years
label: Three years
rationale: The incisor tooth will erupt approximately [LABEL] after enamel mineralization is complete. This conclusion is consistent with known developmental timelines for human incisors.

question:
A 9-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department because of progressively worsening shortness of breath for 3 days. He has had fever and malaise for the past 5 days. He had a sore throat 3 weeks ago that resolved without treatment. He appears ill. His temperature is 38.6°C (101.5°F), pulse is 98/min and blood pressure is 84/62 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 93%. Examination shows jugular venous distension and bilateral ankle edema. There are erythematous, ring-shaped macules and patches over his trunk that are well-demarcated. Auscultation of the chest shows crackles at the lung bases bilaterally. An S3 is heard on cardiac auscultation. His hemoglobin concentration is 12.2 g/dL, leukocyte count is 13,600/mm3, and platelet count is 280,000/mm3. A urinalysis is normal. An x-ray of the chest shows cardiac silhouette enlargement with prominent vascular markings in both the lung fields. Which of the following is the most likely etiology of this patient's symptoms?. Answer Choices:
A. Acute rheumatic fever
B. Viral myocarditis
C. Systemic lupus erythematosus
D. Infection with Borrelia burgdorferi
label: Acute rheumatic fever
rationale: The most likely etiology of this patient's symptoms is [LABEL], which has led to rheumatic heart disease and congestive heart failure.

question:
You are working in the emergency room of a children's hospital when a 4-year-old girl is brought in by ambulance due to "difficulty breathing." The patient had been eating lunch on a school field trip when she suddenly complained of abdominal pain. Shortly thereafter, she was noted to have swelling of the lips, a rapidly developing red rash and difficulty breathing. In the ambulance her blood pressure was persistently 80/50 mmHg despite intramuscular epinephrine. In the course of stabilization and work up of the patient, you note an elevated tryptase level. What is the mechanism behind this elevated tryptase level?. Answer Choices:
A. IgG production by plasma cells
B. Cross-linking of IgE on mast cells
C. Antibody-antigen immune complexes
D. Cross-linking of IgG on mast cells
label: Cross-linking of IgE on mast cells
rationale: The elevated total serum tryptase level is due to mast cell activation caused by [LABEL], consistent with anaphylaxis.

question:
A 19-year-old man presents to the clinic complaining of early fatigue and muscle cramps while playing sports. He is fine when walking or doing less intense levels of work. On physical examination, he appears well and the vital signs are normal. Muscle bulk, tone and strength in the proximal muscles are normal. There is no muscle fatigue with repetitive arm grip exercises. After an exercise stress test, his serum creatine kinase (CK) is elevated and lactate level is normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?. Answer Choices:
A. Gaucher disease
B. Tay-Sachs disease
C. McArdle disease (glycogen storage disease)
D. hemochromatosis
label: McArdle disease (glycogen storage disease)
rationale: The most likely diagnosis is McArdle disease (Glycogen Storage Disease Type V), caused by a deficiency in myophosphorylase, leading to impaired glycogen breakdown in muscle. This explains the exercise-induced muscle cramps, elevated CK levels, and normal lactate levels.

question:
A 25-year-old man presents to the emergency department for severe abdominal pain. The patient states that for the past week he has felt fatigued and had a fever. He states that he has had crampy lower abdominal pain and has experienced several bouts of diarrhea. The patient states that his pain is somewhat relieved by defecation. The patient returned from a camping trip 2 weeks ago in the Rocky Mountains. He is concerned that consuming undercooked meats on his trip may have caused this. He admits to consuming beef and chicken cooked over a fire pit. The patient is started on IV fluids and morphine. His temperature is 99.5°F (37.5°C), blood pressure is 130/77 mmHg, pulse is 90/min, respirations are 12/min, and oxygen saturation is 98% on room air. Laboratory studies are ordered and are seen below.

Hemoglobin: 10 g/dL
Hematocrit: 28%
Leukocyte count: 11,500 cells/mm^3 with normal differential
Platelet count: 445,000/mm^3

Serum:
Na+: 140 mEq/L
Cl-: 102 mEq/L
K+: 4.1 mEq/L
HCO3-: 24 mEq/L
BUN: 24 mg/dL
Glucose: 145 mg/dL
Creatinine: 1.4 mg/dL
Ca2+: 9.6 mg/dL
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR): 75 mm/hour

Physical exam is notable for a patient who appears to be uncomfortable. Gastrointestinal (GI) exam is notable for abdominal pain upon palpation. Ear, nose, and throad exam is notable for multiple painful shallow ulcers in the patient’s mouth. Inspection of the patient’s lower extremities reveals a pruritic ring-like lesion. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are within normal limits. Which of the following best describes this patient’s underlying condition?. Answer Choices:
A. Bowel wall spasticity
B. p-ANCA positive autoimmune bowel disease
C. Rectal mucosa outpouching
D. Transmural granulomas in the bowel
label: Transmural granulomas in the bowel
rationale: The patient's condition is best described as Crohn's disease, characterized by [LABEL], which involve the intestine and are associated with systemic inflammatory features such as oral ulcers and cutaneous lesions.

question:
Phelp's sign is seen in:. Answer Choices:
A. Glomus jugulare
B. Vestibular Schawannoma
C. Maniere's disease
D. Neurofibromatosis
label: Glomus jugulare
rationale: Phelp's sign is associated with a [LABEL] neoplasm and is a distinguishing feature that helps differentiate it from a glomus tympanicum. This conclusion aligns with the anatomical and pathological basis of the tumor's behavior.

question:
A farmer presented with a black mole on the check. It increased in size, more than 6mm with sharply defined borders with central black lesion, what could be the diagnosis?. Answer Choices:
A. Acral lentigo melanoma
B. Superficial spreading melanoma
C. Lentigo maligna melanoma
D. Nodular melanoma
label: Superficial spreading melanoma
rationale: The diagnosis is [LABEL], which aligns with the clinical presentation and risk factors. This type of melanoma is the most common and is associated with pre-existing moles and genetic predispositions (e.g., CDKN2A, BRAF mutations). Skin carcinoma in situ is less likely based on the described features.

question:
In Thyrotoxicosis, B-blockers do not control -. Answer Choices:
A. Anxiety
B. Termors
C. Tachycardia
D. Oxygen consumption
label: Oxygen consumption
rationale: Beta blockers, such as propranolol, effectively regulate beta1-adrenergic receptor activity, reducing symptoms like palpitations, tachycardia, anxiety, and heat intolerance in thyrotoxicosis. However, they do not directly control [LABEL] or the metabolic effects of thyroid hormones. Their primary role is symptom management through the reduction of beta-adrenergic tone.

question:
A 59-year-old woman comes to the physician because of left leg swelling that started after a transcontinental flight. A duplex ultrasound of the left leg shows a noncompressible popliteal vein. A drug is prescribed that inhibits the coagulation cascade. Two weeks later, laboratory studies show:
Platelet count 210,000/mm3
Partial thromboplastin time 53 seconds
Prothrombin time 20 seconds
Thrombin time 15 seconds (control: 15 seconds)
Which of the following drugs was most likely prescribed?". Answer Choices:
A. Alteplase
B. Unfractionated heparin
C. Apixaban
D. Low molecular weight heparin
label: Apixaban
rationale: Based on the clinical presentation of DVT, the diagnostic confirmation via ultrasound, and the need for anticoagulation, [LABEL] is a suitable choice for this patient. It directly inhibits factor Xa in the coagulation cascade, aligning with the treatment goals for DVT.

question:
Von Willebrand&;s factor is synthesized in which one of the following -. Answer Choices:
A. Vascular endothelium
B. Macrophages
C. Liver
D. Eosinophils
label: Vascular endothelium
rationale: The [LABEL] is the primary site of Von Willebrand's factor synthesis.

question:
An investigator is studying the genetic profile of an isolated pathogen that proliferates within macrophages. The pathogen contains sulfatide on the surface of its cell wall to prevent fusion of the phagosome and lysosome. She finds that some of the organisms under investigation have mutations in a gene that encodes the enzyme required for synthesis of RNA from a DNA template. The mutations are most likely to reduce the therapeutic effect of which of the following drugs?. Answer Choices:
A. Streptomycin
B. Rifampin
C. Pyrazinamide
D. Levofloxacin
label: Rifampin
rationale: [LABEL] is the drug whose therapeutic effect is most likely reduced by the mutations in the gene encoding the enzyme required for RNA synthesis.

question:
A 62-year-old man with prostate cancer comes to the physician because of low back pain for 2 weeks and a 4.5-kg (10-lb) weight loss. Physical examination shows localized tenderness over the lumbar spine. An x-ray of the lumbar spine shows several osteoblastic lesions at the level of L2 and L4 vertebrae. Microscopic examination of a bone biopsy specimen from the L4 vertebra shows irregular bone trabeculae and star-shaped cells with long, cytoplasmic processes located deep within the lacunae. Exchange of nutrients and waste products between these cells most likely occurs through which of the following structures?. Answer Choices:
A. Macula communicans
B. Zonula occludens
C. Hemidesmosomes
D. Zonula adherens
label: Macula communicans
rationale: The exchange of nutrients and waste products between the star-shaped cells (osteocytes) most likely occurs through [LABEL] (gap junctions).

question:
A 58-year-old woman comes to the physician because of constipation, loss of appetite, and increased urinary frequency for the past 8 weeks. She has a history of hypertension and underwent mastectomy for breast cancer 9 months ago. Her sister has hyperthyroidism and her mother died of complications from breast cancer at the age of 52 years. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Current medications include chlorthalidone. Her temperature is 36.2°C (97.2°F), pulse is 102/min, and blood pressure is 142/88 mm Hg. Physical examination shows dry mucous membranes. Abdominal examination shows mild, diffuse abdominal tenderness to palpation with decreased bowel sounds. Her serum creatinine concentration is 1.2 mg/dL and serum calcium concentration is 12 mg/dL. Serum parathyroid hormone levels are decreased. Which of the following is the most appropriate long-term pharmacotherapy?. Answer Choices:
A. Denosumab
B. Furosemide
C. Zoledronic acid
D. Prednisone
"
label: Zoledronic acid
rationale: The most likely cause of this patient’s hypercalcemia is malignancy-associated hypercalcemia due to her history of breast cancer. [LABEL] is an appropriate long-term pharmacotherapy for managing hypercalcemia in this context, as it effectively reduces calcium levels and addresses the underlying bone resorption.

question:
A girl aged 20 yrs complains of headache while studying. Her vision is found to be normal. All
of the following should be further evaluated except. Answer Choices:
A. Self worth
B. Family history of headache
C. Menstrual history
D. Her interest in studies
label: Self worth
rationale: The most likely cause of the headache is stress or anxiety related to studying, potentially leading to a tension-type headache. Poor posture or muscle tension while studying could also contribute. Hormonal imbalance is a less likely cause unless the headache coincides with specific phases of the menstrual cycle. Depression is unlikely to be the primary cause, as it would manifest more pervasively and not be limited to studying. Further evaluation of her study habits, stress levels, and menstrual history is recommended to confirm the diagnosis.

question:
FIGLU excretion test is used for assessment of deficiency of -. Answer Choices:
A. VitaminBn
B. Niacin
C. Folic acid
D. Pyridoxin
label: Folic acid
rationale: The FIGLU excretion test is used to assess folate deficiency, which can manifest in various biochemical and hematological changes. While the test itself does not directly diagnose cerebral folate deficiency, systemic folate deficiency could potentially be associated with neurological issues, including cerebral folate deficiency. Therefore, the FIGLU excretion test is primarily an indicator of folate deficiency, with possible implications for neurological health.

question:
Superficial Plamar arch is related to the?. Answer Choices:
A. Distal end of fully extended thumb
B. Distal end of fully flexed thumb
C. Proximal end of the fully flexed thumb
D. Proximal end of fully extended thumb
label: Distal end of fully extended thumb
rationale: The superficial palmar arch is anatomically related to the distal border of the fully extended thumb but not directly to the distal phalanx of manual digit 1. Its branches, however, supply blood to the distal phalanx of the thumb. Therefore, the given answer is partially correct but oversimplifies the relationship.

question:
A 37-year-old woman with right lower extremity edema is evaluated because of the sudden onset of shortness of breath and pleuritic chest pain. A diagnosis of pulmonary embolism is made. Which of the following signs, if present on physical examination, would be the most specific indicator of pulmonary arterial hypertension in this patient?. Answer Choices:
A. Increased jugular venous pressure
B. P2 louder than A2
C. Peripheral edema
D. Presence of an S3
label: P2 louder than A2
rationale: An abnormally loud pulmonic component of the second heart sound ([LABEL]) is the most specific indicator of pulmonary arterial hypertension in this patient.

question:
A 52-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 3-month history of upper abdominal pain and nausea that occurs about 3 hours after eating and at night. These symptoms improve with eating. After eating, he often has a feeling of fullness and bloating. He has had several episodes of dark stools over the past month. He has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 40 years and drinks 2 alcoholic beverages daily. He takes no medications. His temperature is 36.4°C (97.5°F), pulse is 80/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Abdominal examination shows epigastric tenderness with no guarding or rebound. Bowel sounds are normal. Which of the following treatments is most appropriate to prevent further complications of the disease in this patient?. Answer Choices:
A. Amoxicillin, clarithromycin, and omeprazole
B. Fundoplication, hiatoplasty, and gastropexy
C. Distal gastrectomy with gastroduodenostomy
D. Intravenous vitamin B12 supplementation
label: Amoxicillin, clarithromycin, and omeprazole
rationale: The most appropriate treatment to prevent further complications in this patient is a combination of [LABEL]. This triple therapy targets the underlying H. pylori infection and promotes ulcer healing.

question:
An 8-year-old boy presents to his primary care pediatrician for routine check-up. During the visit, his mom says that she has noticed yellow bumps on his eyelids and was concerned about whether they were a problem. Upon hearing this concern, the physician inquires about parental health studies and learns that both parents have high cholesterol despite adhering to a statin regimen. Furthermore, other family members have suffered early myocardial infarctions in their 30s. Physical exam reveals flat yellow patches on the patient's eyelids bilaterally as well as hard yellow bumps around the patient's ankles. Based on clinical suspicion an LDL level is obtained and shows a level of 300 mg/dL. What protein is most likely defective in this patient causing these findings?. Answer Choices:
A. Lipoprotein lipase
B. LDL receptor
C. VLDL receptor
D. Apoprotein E
label: LDL receptor
rationale: The most likely defective protein in this patient causing these findings is the [LABEL] (LDLR).

question:
Which of the following statements about inhalation anesthetic agents is wrong?. Answer Choices:
A. Sevoflurane is more potent than isoflurane
B. Sevoflurane is less cardiodepressant than isoflurane
C. Desflurane has lower blood-gas paition coefficient than sevoflurane
D. Sevoflurane has a higher MAC than isoflurane
label: Sevoflurane is more potent than isoflurane
rationale: Sevoflurane is less potent than isoflurane because it has a higher MAC value, not due to its lack of coronary vasodilating properties. The given answer incorrectly attributes the difference in potency to coronary vasodilation, which is unrelated to the anesthetic potency.

question:
A 4-year-old boy is brought to his pediatrician by his mother for a physical exam before summer camp. They have no complaints or concerns at this time. He was born at 37 weeks gestation by cesarean delivery. The delivery was complicated by an omphalocele and macrosomia. During infancy and into early childhood, he struggled to breathe and eat due to an enlarged tongue. Growth and development were mostly normal with mild uneven growth of his body. He has one uncle that had similar symptoms and is alive and well. The child is up to date on all vaccines and is meeting developmental goals. He enjoys school and playing with his friends. His heart rate of 90/min, respiratory rate of 22/min, blood pressure of 110/65 mm Hg, and temperature of 36.9°C (98.4°F). Overall the child appears healthy. Physical exam findings include known hemihypertrophy of the right side along with a mildly enlarged tongue. This patient is at increased risk of developing which of the following?. Answer Choices:
A. Scoliosis
B. Alzheimer's disease
C. Wilms tumor
D. Sudden infant death syndrome
label: Wilms tumor
rationale: This patient is at increased risk of developing [LABEL], most likely due to Beckwith-Wiedemann Syndrome.

question:
Opening of mouth is caused by:. Answer Choices:
A. Lateral pterygoid
B. Medial pterygoid
C. Temporalis
D. Masseter
label: Lateral pterygoid
rationale: The [LABEL] muscle is the primary muscle responsible for opening the jaw, with assistance from the digastric, mylohyoid, and geniohyoid muscles.

question:
Phophocreatine resynthesis during recovery from exercise is inhibited by:. Answer Choices:
A. an excess of creatine.
B. hyperventilation.
C. an excess of oxygen.
D. a lack of oxygen.
label: a lack of oxygen
rationale: [LABEL] inhibits phosphocreatine resynthesis during recovery from exercise. This is because oxygen is essential for efficient ATP production via oxidative metabolism, which drives the resynthesis process. Hypoxia further exacerbates this inhibition by shifting metabolism toward less efficient anaerobic pathways.

question:
A 32-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of abdominal pain, a runny nose, and chills for 6 hours. He has also had diarrhea and difficulty sleeping. He appears irritable. His temperature is 37.1°C (98.8°F), pulse is 110/min, and blood pressure is 140/90 mm Hg. Examination shows cool, damp skin with piloerection. The pupils are 7 mm in diameter and equal in size. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. The abdomen is tender to palpation. Bowel sounds are hyperactive. Deep tendon reflexes are 3+ bilaterally. Withdrawal from which of the following substances is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?. Answer Choices:
A. Gamma-hydroxybutyric acid
B. Cannabis
C. Phencyclidine
D. Heroin
label: Heroin
rationale: The most likely cause of this patient's symptoms is withdrawal from narcotics, such as [LABEL]. The combination of gastrointestinal hyperactivity, autonomic symptoms (rhinorrhea, chills, hyperhidrosis), sleep disturbance, irritability, and physical findings (piloerection, hyperactive reflexes) strongly supports this diagnosis.

question:
A patient is a known case of polyarteritis nodosa. On examination of biopsy, accumulation of amorphous, basic, proteinaceous material in the vessel wall was seen. This finding is suggestive of:. Answer Choices:
A. Fibrinoid necrosis
B. Leucocytoclastic vasculitis
C. Hyaline arteriosclerosis
D. Caseous necrosis
label: Fibrinoid necrosis
rationale: The finding of amorphous, basic, proteinaceous material in the vessel wall is indicative of [LABEL], a hallmark of polyarteritis nodosa. This process is distinct from hyaline arteriosclerosis due to the fibrin-like quality of the deposited material.

question:
Most sensitive indicator of iron deficiency anemia. Answer Choices:
A. Packed cell volume
B. Hemoglobin
C. Serum ferritin
D. Serum iron
label: Serum ferritin
rationale: Based on the reasoning process, [LABEL] is indeed the most sensitive indicator of iron deficiency anemia. It reflects the body's iron stores and decreases early in the course of iron deficiency, even before anemia is clinically apparent.

question:
A 27-year-old gravida 2, para 1 presents to her physician at 21 weeks gestation with decreased sensitivity, tingling, and pain in her right hand that is worse at night and is partially relieved by shaking her hand. She developed these symptoms gradually over the past month. She does not report any trauma to her extremities, neck, or spine. The physical examination shows a normal range of motion of the neck, spine, and extremities. On neurologic examination, the patient has 2+ biceps and triceps reflexes. She has decreased pressure and temperature sensitivity over the palmar surface of the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd fingers. Wrist flexion and tapping the skin over the flexor retinaculum trigger exacerbation of the symptoms. Which of the following statements about the patient’s condition is correct?. Answer Choices:
A. This is a fairly uncommon condition in pregnant women.
B. Pre-pregnancy obesity increases risk of developing this condition during pregnancy.
C. Corticosteroid injections are contraindicated in pregnant women for management of this condition.
D. Immobilization (for example, splinting) should improve the reported outcome in this patient.
label: Immobilization (for example, splinting) should improve the reported outcome in this patient
rationale: The patient's symptoms are consistent with carpal tunnel syndrome, likely exacerbated by pregnancy. Immobilization through wrist splinting is an appropriate and effective initial treatment to improve the reported symptoms.

question:
A 38-year-old woman presents to a physician’s office for progressive weakness and pallor during the last few weeks. She also complains of shortness of breath during her yoga class. She denies fevers, cough, rhinorrhea, or changes in appetite or bowel and bladder habits. She is generally healthy except for an occasional migraine, which is relieved by acetaminophen. For the last month, she has been having more frequent migraine attacks and was started on prophylactic aspirin. The vital signs include: pulse 102/min, respirations 18/min, and blood pressure 130/84 mm Hg. Her blood pressure on previous visits has been 110/76 mm Hg, 120/78 mm Hg, and 114/80 mm Hg. The physical examination is otherwise unremarkable. Stool for occult blood is positive. In addition to a low hemoglobin concentration, which other laboratory finding is expected in this patient?. Answer Choices:
A. Decreased platelet count
B. Elevated D-dimer
C. Prolonged prothrombin time (PT)
D. Prolonged bleeding time
label: Prolonged bleeding time
rationale: The patient is expected to have [LABEL] due to aspirin-induced platelet inhibition, compounded by potential platelet dysfunction from iron deficiency anemia. This aligns with the clinical findings of low hemoglobin and a positive stool occult blood test.

question:
A 47–year-old man presents to the emergency department with worsening weakness in the setting of persistent abdominal pain. The man arrived to the United States 6 months ago and has been working in a restaurant as a cook. His abdominal pain started 4 months ago, but he could not find time away from work to see a doctor. He reports nausea but denies any vomiting. His temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 98/61 mmHg, pulse is 110/min, and respirations are 18/min. He has no cardiac murmurs but does have tenderness in his epigastric region. His heme-occult test is positive. His laboratory workup notes the following:

Hemoglobin: 7.2 g/dL
Hematocrit: 23%
Leukocyte count: 11,000/mm^3 with normal differential
Platelet count: 470,000/mm^3

Serum:
Na+: 137 mEq/L
Cl-: 109 mEq/L
K+: 3.1 mEq/L
HCO3-: 23 mEq/L
BUN: 52 mg/dL
Glucose: 89 mg/dL
Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL

An esophagogastroduodenoscopy reveals the presence of a mass surrounded by bleeding ulcers. On initial shave biopsy, the preliminary diagnosis is mucosa-associated lymphatic tissue lymphoma (MALToma). What is the best next step in management?. Answer Choices:
A. Amoxicillin, clarithromycin, and pantoprazole
B. Full thickness biopsy
C. Hospice care
D. Partial gastrectomy
label: Amoxicillin, clarithromycin, and pantoprazole
rationale: The best next step in management is treatment with [LABEL], which targets the eradication of H. pylori, potentially leading to regression of the gastric MALToma.

question:
A first-year medical student is analyzing data in a nationwide cancer registry.
